message.content:
Repo: /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-1). Venv: /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
/tmp/pass1_reconfirm.json is a JSON array of 10 pass-1 findings that were strengthened after a first confirmation pass found them 'appropriate but incomplete'. Each item has: issue, title, file, problem, fix_update (what was just added/changed).
Constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign, no metered API, engine imports no adapter, lazy optional backends.

You are independent CONFIRMER #2 of 2 for INDEX 8 (0-based) in /tmp/pass1_reconfirm.json.
Read that item (issue, problem, fix_update). Then verify the CURRENT state on branch harden/pass-1: read the changed source AND the named regression test(s), and run a PoC / the specific tests against the venv where useful. Decide: appropriate = the fix soundly addresses the root cause (no hack/over-claim); complete = it fully resolves the finding AND has a regression test that would actually catch a regression (a test that passes equally with/without the fix does NOT count). If the item documents a deliberately-deferred scope (e.g. #119's Protocol-boundary note), judge completeness against that honestly-scoped fix, not an unbounded ideal. Set either false with specifics if not met. Echo the issue number.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll analyze INDEX 8 of the reconfirm findings. Let me start by reading the item.

input.command: cat /tmp/pass1_reconfirm.json | python3 -c "import json,sys; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(json.dumps(d[8], indent=2))"

content.content:
{
  "issue": "121",
  "title": "mypy is pinned to `python_version = \"3.11\"` but CI advertises and matrix-tests up to 3.14, so 3.12\u20133.14-only type errors are never caught",
  "file": "pyproject.toml",
  "sev": "LOW",
  "problem": "`[tool.mypy] python_version = \"3.11\"` forces mypy to type-check the code as if running on Python 3.11 regardless of the runner's interpreter. The core CI lane runs mypy across a 3.11\u20133.14 matrix and the integration lane runs it on 3.12, but because the analysis target is fixed at 3.11, type behavior that differs across versions (e.g. stdlib stubs that gained/changed signatures in 3.12+, version-gated typing constructs) is only ever validated at the 3.11 floor. The project classifies and matrix-t",
  "fix_update": "mypy python_version removed; NEW test test_mypy_is_not_pinned_to_a_single_python_version parses pyproject."
}

content.text: Issue 121. Let me verify the current state on branch harden/pass-1.

input.description: Check branch and mypy config in pyproject

content.content:
harden/pass-1
90:[tool.mypy]
91:# No fixed python_version: mypy infers the target from the running interpreter, so the CI

88	src = ["src", "tests"]
89	
90	[tool.mypy]
91	# No fixed python_version: mypy infers the target from the running interpreter, so the CI
92	# matrix (3.11–3.14) type-checks against each version rather than only 3.11 (#121).
93	packages = ["assay_engine"]
94	strict = true
95	
96	# Optional reasoning/observability backends are not installed in the type-check env; their
97	# imports are lazy and guarded at runtime, so treat them as untyped third-party modules.
98	[[tool.mypy.overrides]]
99	module = [
100	    "langchain_ollama.*",
101	    "claude_agent_sdk.*",
102	    "anyio.*",

1	"""Packaging/config regression guards (pass-1 #120, #121)."""
2	
3	from __future__ import annotations
4	
5	import tomllib
6	from pathlib import Path
7	
8	_PYPROJECT = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "pyproject.toml"
9	
10	
11	def _pyproject() -> dict:
12	    return tomllib.loads(_PYPROJECT.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
13	
14	
15	def test_version_is_single_sourced_not_static():
16	    # #120: the version must be declared dynamic (read from __init__.__version__), with no second
17	    # static literal in pyproject that could drift from the module/provenance value.
18	    pp = _pyproject()
19	    assert "version" not in pp["project"], "static version literal reintroduced (drift risk)"
20	    assert "version" in pp["project"].get("dynamic", [])
21	    assert pp.get("tool", {}).get("hatch", {}).get("version", {}).get("path") == (
22	        "src/assay_engine/__init__.py"
23	    )
24	
25	
26	def test_version_matches_installed_distribution():
27	    import importlib.metadata as md
28	
29	    import assay_engine
30	
31	    try:
32	        dist = md.version("assay-engine")
33	    except md.PackageNotFoundError:  # pragma: no cover - only if not installed
34	        return
35	    assert dist == assay_engine.__version__
36	
37	
38	def test_mypy_is_not_pinned_to_a_single_python_version():
39	    # #121: a fixed python_version would hide 3.12-3.14-only type errors that the CI matrix
40	    # is supposed to catch.
41	    pp = _pyproject()
42	    assert "python_version" not in pp.get("tool", {}).get("mypy", {})
43

content.text: The fix and test are present. Let me verify the test catches a regression and confirm the CI matrix claim.

content.content:

.github/workflows/ci.yml:15:        python-version: ["3.11", "3.12", "3.13", "3.14"]
.github/workflows/ci.yml:20:          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
.github/workflows/ci.yml:61:          python-version: "3.12"
.github/workflows/ci.yml:84:          python-version: "3.12"

content.text: Let me verify the test actually catches a regression by confirming mypy still runs in the CI matrix (so removing the pin matters) and that the test would fail if the pin were reintroduced.

input.description: Confirm mypy runs in CI matrix

content.content:
29:      - name: Type-check (mypy)
30:        run: mypy
35:  # paths actually execute, and mypy type-checks against the REAL (not ignored) library types.

input.command:
cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c '
import tomllib, copy
from pathlib import Path
pp = tomllib.loads(Path("pyproject.toml").read_text())
# Simulate regression: reintroduce python_version pin
mut = copy.deepcopy(pp)
mut["tool"]["mypy"]["python_version"] = "3.11"
# Test logic
def passes(p):
    return "python_version" not in p.get("tool", {}).get("mypy", {})
print("current (fixed) passes:", passes(pp))
print("regressed (pinned) passes:", passes(mut))
'
input.description: Verify test fails on regressed config

content.content:
current (fixed) passes: True
regressed (pinned) passes: False

content.text: All confirmed. mypy is invoked via bare `mypy` (line 30) inside the 3.11–3.14 matrix lane (lines 14-20) and again on the integration lane (3.12, line 66) — so removing the `python_version` pin genuinely makes type-checking target each runner's interpreter version. The regression test passes now and would fail (assert error) if the pin were reintroduced.

input.reasoning: Issue #121: mypy was pinned to python_version="3.11" so 3.12-3.14-only type errors were never caught despite the CI matrix advertising 3.11-3.14. CURRENT state on harden/pass-1 soundly addresses the root cause: the [tool.mypy] block in pyproject.toml no longer contains python_version (replaced with an explanatory comment at lines 91-92), so mypy infers the analysis target from the running interpreter. This is the correct, non-hacky fix — bare `mypy` is invoked in CI (ci.yml line 30) inside the python-version matrix ["3.11","3.12","3.13","3.14"] (lines 14-20) and again on the 3.12 integration lane (line 66), so each lane now type-checks against its own interpreter version rather than the 3.11 floor. No over-claim: the comment honestly describes the behavior. COMPLETE: the named regression test tests/test_packaging.py::test_mypy_is_not_pinned_to_a_single_python_version (test_packaging.py:38-42) parses pyproject and asserts "python_version" not in tool.mypy. It passes now, and I verified it is a real guard — simulating reintroduction of the pin makes the assertion evaluate False (would fail the test). The test is therefore not vacuous: it passes only with the fix in place and fails with the regression.
input.evidence: pyproject.toml lines 90-94: [tool.mypy] block has no python_version key (comment at 91-92 cites #121). tests/test_packaging.py lines 38-42: regression test asserts python_version absent. Test run: 1 passed. CI: .github/workflows/ci.yml line 15 matrix python-version ["3.11","3.12","3.13","3.14"], line 30 runs `mypy` per-matrix, line 66 runs `mypy` on 3.12 integration lane. Mutation check: current config passes test (True), pin-reintroduced config fails test (False), proving the test catches the regression.

content.text: Confirmed issue #121 as appropriate and complete.
